events.text: The patient is a 52-year-old woman named Margaret Chen. She is a never-smoker with no significant alcohol use history. She works as a high school music teacher. She has no significant family history of head and neck cancer. She is of East Asian descent, married, and lives with her spouse and two adult children.

At age 51 years and 9 months, the patient presented to her primary care physician with a 3-month history of persistent left-sided throat discomfort and sensation of a foreign body in the oropharynx. She reported mild odynophagia but no dysphagia to solid foods, no hoarseness, no weight loss, and no otalgia. Examination by the primary care physician revealed mild erythema of the posterior pharyngeal wall. She was treated empirically for pharyngitis with amoxicillin-clavulanate and throat lozenges. Symptoms did not resolve. No cancer identified at this visit.

At age 51 years and 11 months, the patient returned to her primary care physician with ongoing left throat discomfort now accompanied by mild left-sided ear pain. On examination, a firm, non-tender left level II cervical lymph node approximately 2 cm in diameter was noted. She was referred urgently to otolaryngology. No cancer confirmed at this time, but malignancy suspected.

At age 51 years and 11 months, the patient had a CT scan of the neck with contrast, which showed a 2.1 x 1.8 cm left level IIA cervical lymph node with internal heterogeneity and loss of fatty hilum, suspicious for metastatic disease. Additionally, a subtle asymmetric fullness of the left base of tongue was noted. No contralateral lymphadenopathy. Impression: findings highly suspicious for primary oropharyngeal malignancy with ipsilateral nodal metastasis. Cancer present.

At age 52 years, the patient was evaluated by otolaryngology/head and neck surgery. Flexible nasopharyngoscopy revealed an exophytic, friable lesion arising from the left base of tongue measuring approximately 2 cm. The lesion bled on contact. The oropharynx was otherwise unremarkable. Bimanual palpation confirmed the left level II lymph node. The patient was counseled regarding findings and scheduled for biopsy under anesthesia. Cancer present, not yet confirmed histologically.

At age 52 years, the patient underwent direct laryngoscopy with biopsy of the left base of tongue lesion. Pathology result: squamous cell carcinoma, moderately differentiated, arising from left base of tongue. Immunohistochemistry demonstrated diffuse strong p16 overexpression (block positive, >70% of tumor cells), consistent with HPV-related oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma. Tumor cells were positive for CK5/6 and p40. Ki-67 proliferation index was approximately 60%. HPV in situ hybridization confirmed high-risk HPV (type 16) within tumor cells. Fine needle aspiration of the left level IIA lymph node was also performed and showed metastatic squamous cell carcinoma, p16 positive.

At age 52 years, the patient had a diagnosis of Stage III (T2N1M0, AJCC 8th edition for HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma) of the BASE OF TONGUE (ICD-O-3 site code C01.9), histology: SQUAMOUS CELL CARCINOMA, MODERATELY DIFFERENTIATED (ICD-O-3 histology code 8070/3). Primary tumor: T2 (greatest dimension >2 cm but ≤4 cm; 2.1 cm at greatest dimension). Regional lymph nodes: N1 (one ipsilateral lymph node, ≤6 cm; single left level IIA node 2.1 cm). Distant metastasis: M0. AJCC 8th edition stage group: III (HPV-positive staging system). Summary stage: Regional. Laterality: Left. Biomarkers: p16 positive (IHC, strongly and diffusely positive); HPV-16 positive (ISH confirmed). Tobacco history: Never smoker (pack-year history = 0). Alcohol use: Minimal (< 1 drink/week). ECOG performance status: 1. Confirmation: Positive histology via direct laryngoscopy biopsy and FNA of lymph node.

At age 52 years, the patient had a PET-CT scan (18F-FDG) for staging, which showed a hypermetabolic left base of tongue lesion (SUVmax 12.4), consistent with the known primary malignancy. A single hypermetabolic left level IIA cervical lymph node (SUVmax 9.8, 2.1 cm short axis) was identified. No contralateral cervical nodal involvement. No distant metastatic disease. No evidence of synchronous primary malignancy. Cancer present. No distant metastases.

At age 52 years, the patient had an MRI of the neck with and without contrast for pre-treatment planning, which showed a T2-hyperintense left base of tongue mass measuring 2.2 x 1.9 x 1.7 cm with avid gadolinium enhancement. No extension to the vallecula or pre-epiglottic space. No involvement of the midline or right base of tongue. A left level IIA lymph node measured 2.1 x 1.8 cm with central necrosis. No extranodal extension radiographically. No other suspicious lymphadenopathy. Cancer present. Primary confined to left base of tongue.

At age 52 years, the patient had next-generation sequencing performed for HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma based on a specimen obtained from the primary tumor (left base of tongue biopsy). Sequencing platform: 500-gene targeted panel. Results showed: PIK3CA p.E545K mutation (exon 9, hotspot, variant allele frequency 28%, likely oncogenic); TRAF3 p.R242* truncating mutation (VAF 41%, likely pathogenic, associated with HPV-positive HNSCC); KMT2D p.Q1555* truncating mutation (VAF 35%, loss of function); EP300 p.S1415fs*14 frameshift mutation (VAF 19%, loss of function); FBXW7 p.R465C mutation (VAF 22%, likely oncogenic); FAT1 p.E2386* truncating mutation (VAF 31%); NOTCH1 p.R2382* truncating mutation (VAF 18%, loss of function, likely tumor suppressor); TP53 wild-type (consistent with HPV-positive HNSCC); KRAS wild-type; EGFR wild-type; CDKN2A wild-type. Copy number alterations: PIK3CA amplification (chromosome 3q26, low-level gain); CCND1 wild-type (no amplification); no focal high-level amplifications. No actionable fusions identified. TMB: 7.2 mutations/Mb (intermediate). MSI: stable. PD-L1 CPS: 18. No EGFR amplification. No FGFR alterations. Overall mutational profile consistent with HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma.

At age 52 years, the patient was presented at the multidisciplinary head and neck tumor board. Consensus recommendation: definitive concurrent cisplatin-based chemoradiation with intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT). Surgery was not recommended given T2N1 HPV-positive disease with favorable prognosis with non-surgical management. De-escalation trial enrollment was discussed but patient was deemed ineligible due to the PIK3CA mutation potentially affecting prognosis; standard cisplatin-based concurrent chemoradiation was recommended. Goals of treatment: curative intent. Dental evaluation completed prior to radiation. Audiogram obtained as baseline prior to cisplatin: normal hearing bilaterally. Baseline creatinine 0.78 mg/dL, GFR >60 mL/min. Pre-treatment weight: 62 kg. Cancer present.

At age 52 years, the patient was evaluated by speech-language pathology prior to initiation of chemoradiation. Baseline swallowing function assessed: normal oral phase, mild pharyngeal pooling on modified barium swallow study. Patient was counseled extensively on expected toxicities including mucositis, dysphagia, xerostomia, and radiation dermatitis. Prophylactic PEG tube placement was discussed and the patient elected to defer, agreeing to re-evaluate during treatment if oral intake became inadequate. Nutritional baseline: BMI 22.1 kg/m2, albumin 4.1 g/dL. No cancer confirmed or excluded at this visit.

At age 52 years, the patient began definitive intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT) to the oropharynx and bilateral neck. Treatment plan: 70 Gy in 35 fractions to the primary tumor and gross nodal disease (high-risk PTV), 63 Gy in 35 fractions to the high-risk elective nodal regions, 56 Gy in 35 fractions to the low-risk elective nodal volumes. Field: bilateral neck included; ipsilateral level IB, II, III, IV, V and retropharyngeal nodes, and contralateral level II, III nodes. Technique: step-and-shoot IMRT with daily image guidance (CBCT). Concurrent cisplatin 100 mg/m2 IV every 3 weeks (weeks 1, 4, and 7 of radiation). Curative intent. Parotid glands spared to the extent feasible; mean parotid dose approximately 24 Gy bilaterally.

At age 52 years, the patient received curative-intent concurrent cisplatin 100 mg/m2 IV every 3 weeks as a radiosensitizer during definitive IMRT. Cycle 1 administered on day 1 of radiation. Pre-medications included ondansetron, dexamethasone, and aggressive IV hydration. Cycle 1 completed without dose modification. Baseline creatinine 0.78 mg/dL. Patient tolerated cycle 1 with grade 1 nausea and fatigue.

At age 52 years (approximately week 3 of chemoradiation), the patient developed grade 2 mucositis involving the oropharynx and base of tongue, grade 2 radiation dermatitis of the bilateral neck, and grade 1 dysgeusia. She reported increasing odynophagia with oral intake reduced to soft foods and nutritional supplements. Weight had declined 3 kg from baseline. Management: aggressive oral hygiene protocol, magic mouthwash, viscous lidocaine, sodium bicarbonate rinses. Nutritional supplementation with Ensure Plus. Pain managed with acetaminophen and low-dose oxycodone as needed.

At age 52 years (approximately week 5 of chemoradiation), the patient developed grade 3 mucositis with confluent pseudomembranous changes throughout the oropharynx and supraglottis. She reported severe odynophagia and was unable to maintain adequate oral intake (less than 500 kcal/day). Weight loss totaled 6.5 kg from baseline (10.5% body weight loss). Grade 2 radiation dermatitis with moist desquamation of the bilateral anterior neck was also noted. She required IV hydration on two occasions. Decision made to proceed with percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG) tube placement for nutritional support. PEG tube was placed without complication. Enteral nutrition initiated: Jevity 1.5 at 60 mL/hour overnight plus daytime bolus feedings, providing approximately 1800 kcal/day. Opioid analgesics escalated to around-the-clock oxycodone with breakthrough dosing.

At age 52 years (week 4 of radiation), the patient received cycle 2 of cisplatin 100 mg/m2 IV with concurrent IMRT. Creatinine remained stable at 0.84 mg/dL. Full dose administered. Patient experienced grade 2 nausea managed with ondansetron and prochlorperazine. Grade 1 tinnitus noted; audiogram deferred until end of treatment. Patient completed cycle 2 without dose reduction.

At age 52 years (week 6 of chemoradiation), the patient developed grade 2 nephrotoxicity with creatinine rising to 1.32 mg/dL (from baseline 0.78 mg/dL). Aggressive IV hydration was administered. Creatinine improved to 1.05 mg/dL over 5 days. Grade 1 peripheral neuropathy (mild tingling in fingertips) was noted. Electrolytes: hypomagnesemia (Mg 1.3 mEq/L) requiring IV magnesium repletion. Due to creatinine elevation, cycle 3 of cisplatin was held. Total cisplatin: 2 of 3 planned cycles administered (cumulative dose 340 mg/m2). IMRT was continued without interruption and completed as planned.

At age 52 years, the patient completed definitive IMRT to 70 Gy in 35 fractions over 7 weeks (concurrent with cisplatin). Final fraction delivered without interruption. Total delivered dose to primary: 70 Gy (35 fractions). High-risk elective nodes: 63 Gy (35 fractions). Low-risk elective nodes: 56 Gy (35 fractions). No treatment breaks. Radiation oncology assessment at completion: grade 3 mucositis resolving, grade 2 radiation dermatitis, PEG tube in situ. Treatment completed with curative intent.

At age 52 years (4 weeks post-completion of chemoradiation), the patient was seen in follow-up. Grade 3 mucositis had improved to grade 1 (erythema without ulceration). She was slowly resuming oral intake of liquids and soft foods, taking approximately 800-1000 kcal orally per day. PEG tube supplementation ongoing. Weight stabilized at 55 kg (7 kg below baseline). Grade 1 xerostomia present. Grade 1 peripheral neuropathy persisted in fingertips. Creatinine returned to 0.85 mg/dL. Audiogram showed mild high-frequency hearing loss bilaterally (4 kHz and 8 kHz), grade 1 ototoxicity. No clinical evidence of residual disease on flexible laryngoscopy. Cancer status: likely responding, awaiting formal restaging imaging.

At age 52 years (12 weeks post-completion of chemoradiation), the patient had a PET-CT scan for post-treatment restaging, which showed marked interval decrease in FDG avidity at the left base of tongue (previously SUVmax 12.4, now SUVmax 1.8, consistent with post-treatment change/complete metabolic response). The left level IIA lymph node had decreased markedly in size (now 0.9 cm, SUVmax 1.4, no pathologic FDG uptake). No new hypermetabolic lesions identified. No distant metastatic disease. Impression: Complete metabolic response at primary site and regional lymph nodes. No cancer detected.

At age 52 years (12 weeks post-chemoradiation), the patient was seen in multidisciplinary follow-up. PET-CT demonstrated complete metabolic response. Flexible nasopharyngoscopy: no visible residual tumor at left base of tongue; mucosa appeared smooth and healing. No palpable lymphadenopathy. The patient was continuing PEG tube feedings as primary nutrition source, with oral intake of soft/liquid diet. Speech-language pathology evaluation confirmed improved swallowing function; PEG tube decannulation planned within 4-8 weeks pending adequate oral intake. Patient was counseled on complete response and surveillance plan. Surveillance to include clinical examination every 2 months for first year, then every 3 months in year 2. Annual CT or PET-CT imaging. Post-treatment hypothyroidism screening: TSH 2.8 mIU/L (normal). No cancer detected.

At age 52 years and 6 months (approximately 6 months post-completion of chemoradiation), the patient was seen for surveillance. She reported significantly improved swallowing; PEG tube was removed 8 weeks prior. She was tolerating a near-normal diet with occasional difficulty with dry or hard foods. Weight: 58 kg (3 kg below pre-treatment baseline but stable). Ongoing grade 1 xerostomia managed with artificial saliva and frequent water sips. Grade 1 fibrosis of the bilateral anterior neck on examination. No palpable lymphadenopathy. Flexible nasopharyngoscopy: no evidence of recurrent or residual disease at the left base of tongue or elsewhere. TSH: 4.2 mIU/L (upper normal; monitoring). Discussion of de-escalation considerations: patient was informed of the favorable prognosis of HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC and ongoing trials of de-escalation (reduced radiation dose or omission of chemotherapy). Given complete response to standard treatment, continued surveillance recommended with no additional adjuvant therapy. No cancer detected.

At age 52 years and 6 months, the patient had a CT scan of the neck with contrast for 6-month post-treatment surveillance, which showed no evidence of recurrent primary oropharyngeal malignancy. The previously noted left level IIA lymph node was no longer enlarged (now 0.7 cm, within normal limits). Post-radiation changes including mucosal thickening of the oropharynx and cervical soft tissue fibrosis were noted. No new lymphadenopathy. No cancer detected.

At age 52 years and 9 months (9 months post-completion of chemoradiation), routine surveillance visit. Patient doing well overall. Swallowing function nearly normal, eating a general diet with the exception of very dry foods. Grade 1 xerostomia persistent but improved compared to earlier assessments. No new complaints. No palpable lymphadenopathy. Flexible nasopharyngoscopy: no evidence of recurrent disease. TSH: 5.8 mIU/L (mildly elevated); endocrinology referral placed for consideration of levothyroxine. ECOG performance status 1. Patient returned to part-time work as a music teacher. No cancer detected.

At age 53 years (12 months post-completion of chemoradiation, approximately 1-year follow-up), comprehensive annual surveillance evaluation. Patient reports excellent quality of life. Dysphagia resolved, tolerating a normal diet. Xerostomia improved to intermittent and mild. Hypothyroidism confirmed: TSH 7.1 mIU/L, free T4 0.8 ng/dL; levothyroxine 50 mcg daily initiated. Physical examination: no palpable cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy. Flexible nasopharyngoscopy: healed left base of tongue mucosa without lesion. No signs of recurrence. Discussion of de-escalation trials: patient was again counseled on emerging data supporting de-escalated treatment for HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC in future patients; however, she has already completed standard treatment and achieved complete response. Long-term surveillance plan discussed. Survivorship care plan provided. No cancer detected.

At age 53 years (12 months post-chemoradiation), the patient had a PET-CT scan for 1-year post-treatment surveillance, which showed no FDG-avid disease at the primary site, regional lymph nodes, or distant sites. Post-radiation changes in the oropharynx and bilateral neck were stable compared to prior imaging. No evidence of recurrent or metastatic disease. No cancer detected.

At age 53 years and 6 months (18 months post-completion of chemoradiation), surveillance visit. Patient continues to do very well. No symptoms of recurrence. Levothyroxine dose optimized to 75 mcg daily; TSH now 1.9 mIU/L (normal). Grade 1 xerostomia is the primary ongoing complaint, managed with hydration and saliva substitutes. Physical examination: no lymphadenopathy, no palpable mass in the neck or oropharynx. Flexible nasopharyngoscopy: no evidence of recurrent disease. ECOG performance status 0. Patient returned to full-time teaching. No cancer detected.

At age 54 years (24 months post-completion of chemoradiation), 2-year surveillance visit. Patient reports feeling well. No new symptoms. Examination unremarkable. No lymphadenopathy. Flexible nasopharyngoscopy negative. The patient was counseled that 2-year follow-up without recurrence is a very favorable milestone for HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC and that the risk of late recurrence is low. Annual surveillance to continue with clinical examination every 6 months and imaging as clinically indicated. TSH stable on levothyroxine 75 mcg. The patient is engaged in a cancer survivorship program and is an advocate for HPV vaccination in her school community. No cancer detected.

At age 54 years (24 months post-chemoradiation), the patient had a CT scan of the neck with contrast for 2-year post-treatment surveillance, which showed no evidence of recurrent primary oropharyngeal malignancy or regional lymph node metastasis. Stable post-treatment changes. No new findings. No cancer detected.

documents.text:
PRIMARY CARE FOLLOW-UP NOTE

ATTENDING PHYSICIAN: [Primary Care Physician]
DEPARTMENT: Internal Medicine / Primary Care
VISIT TYPE: Return Office Visit

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Return visit for persistent left-sided throat pain, now with new left ear pain. Also noting a new lump in the left side of the neck.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Margaret Chen is a 51-year-old woman (East Asian descent, never-smoker) who returns today approximately 6-8 weeks after her last visit, at which time she was seen for a 3-month history of left-sided throat discomfort and a sensation of something stuck in her throat. At that prior visit she was treated empirically for presumed pharyngitis with a course of amoxicillin-clavulanate. She reports completing the full course of antibiotics, but her symptoms did not improve. She denies any interim fevers, chills, or sore throat in the acute infectious sense.

Today she presents with the following new and evolving concerns:

1. Persistent left-sided throat discomfort: ongoing, essentially unchanged from her prior visit. She describes it as a deep, dull ache in the left posterior throat that is always present and worsens slightly with swallowing. She had brief partial improvement early in the antibiotic course, but symptoms fully returned and are now at the same level as before.

2. New left-sided otalgia: she reports a new, intermittent dull ache in the left ear that she estimates began approximately 2-3 weeks ago. She denies any ear discharge, hearing loss, sensation of fullness in the ear, or recent upper respiratory tract infection. She has not had ear pain in the past and has no history of chronic otitis media. She notes that the ear pain seems to be worse when she swallows.

3. New left neck lump: approximately 2 weeks ago she noticed a painless lump in the left side of her neck, just below the angle of the jaw. She describes it as firm and non-tender and states it has not changed in size since she first noticed it. She initially attributed it to a swollen gland, but is concerned that it has not resolved.

She continues to have mild odynophagia, particularly to swallowing saliva and liquids at times. She denies frank dysphagia to solid foods at this time, though she has not been eating heartily because of throat discomfort. No hoarseness. No voice change. No shortness of breath. No trismus. No epistaxis or blood-tinged sputum. No drooling. She specifically denies any unintentional weight loss, night sweats, or fevers.

She has had no sick contacts. No known exposures to streptococcal illness. No recent dental procedures or dental pain. She does not recall any prior episodes of similar lymphadenopathy.

She works as a high school music teacher and notes she finds it increasingly difficult to project her voice in the classroom due to the throat discomfort.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
- Constitutional: No fevers, no chills, no documented weight loss, no night sweats, appetite slightly reduced due to odynophagia but no significant weight change reported.
- HEENT: Left-sided otalgia as above. No hearing loss. No nasal discharge or congestion. No epistaxis. No oral lesions noticed by patient. No changes in voice quality. No vision changes.
- Neck: New left neck mass as described.
- Respiratory: No cough, no shortness of breath, no hemoptysis.
- GI: No dysphagia to solids at this time. Mild odynophagia to liquids. No nausea, vomiting, or reflux.
- Neurological: No facial numbness or weakness. No headaches. No tongue weakness or deviation.
- Lymphatic: Positive for left cervical adenopathy. No noted axillary or inguinal swelling.
- All other systems reviewed and negative.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
- No prior malignancy
- No history of chronic tonsillitis or recurrent pharyngitis
- No known GERD or acid reflux
- No thyroid disease
- No significant medical conditions on file

PAST SURGICAL HISTORY:
- No prior surgeries

FAMILY HISTORY:
- No family history of head and neck cancer
- No family history of lymphoma
- No significant cancer history in first-degree relatives per prior documentation

SOCIAL HISTORY:
- Never smoker (lifetime pack-year history: 0)
- Alcohol: rare, less than 1 drink per week
- Occupation: high school music teacher
- Marital status: married; lives with spouse and two adult children
- No recreational drug use
- No known occupational or environmental carcinogen exposures

ALLERGIES:
- No known drug allergies

MEDICATIONS:
- Amoxicillin-clavulanate 875 mg PO BID x 10 days (completed; prescribed at prior visit)
- Occasional OTC throat lozenges
- No other regular medications

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:

Vital Signs: Temperature 36.8°C. Blood pressure 118/74 mmHg. Heart rate 76 bpm, regular. Respiratory rate 14. SpO2 98% on room air. Weight 62 kg.

General: Alert and oriented, well-appearing East Asian woman in no acute distress. Appropriate affect, mildly anxious during the visit.

HEENT:
- Head: Normocephalic, atraumatic.
- Eyes: PERRL, EOM intact, no icterus.
- Ears: External auditory canals clear bilaterally. Tympanic membranes intact and without erythema bilaterally. Left tympanic membrane appears normal without effusion or injection. No tenderness over the tragus or mastoid.
- Nose: Nasal mucosa mildly congested, no polyps. No septal deviation noted.
- Oral cavity: Oropharyngeal mucosa with mild persistent erythema of the left posterior pharyngeal wall, consistent with prior exam. Tonsils 2+, symmetric and without exudate. Limited visualization of the left base of tongue on oral exam. No obvious mucosal ulceration visible on tongue or buccal mucosa. Dentition intact. No trismus.

Neck: Supple. A firm, non-tender, discrete lymph node is palpable in the left level II cervical region (high jugular chain, just below the angle of the mandible) measuring approximately 2 cm in greatest diameter. The node is rubbery-to-firm in consistency, non-mobile, and non-fluctuant. There is no overlying skin erythema, warmth, or tenderness. No right-sided cervical lymphadenopathy. No supraclavicular lymphadenopathy bilaterally. No palpable thyroid enlargement or nodularity.

Lymphatic: As above; left level II lymph node as described. No axillary or inguinal adenopathy appreciated on limited exam.

Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezing, rales, or rhonchi.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended.

Neurological: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. No facial asymmetry. Tongue midline.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS:
No labs drawn at today's visit. Prior CBC from approximately 2 months ago was within normal limits.

---

IMAGING RESULTS:
No prior imaging on file at this time.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

1. LEFT CERVICAL LYMPHADENOPATHY WITH IPSILATERAL OTALGIA AND PERSISTENT PHARYNGEAL DISCOMFORT — URGENT CONCERN FOR MALIGNANCY:

Ms. Chen presents today with a clinical picture that has evolved significantly since her last visit and now raises serious concern for an underlying malignant etiology. The combination of: (a) persistent unilateral throat pain refractory to antibiotics, (b) new referred left otalgia (suggesting cranial nerve IX or X involvement, raising concern for a tonsillar or base of tongue primary), and (c) a firm, non-tender, approximately 2 cm left level II cervical lymph node that is non-mobile — is highly suspicious for a primary head and neck malignancy with regional nodal spread. The differential diagnosis at this juncture includes oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma (particularly base of tongue or tonsillar primary), lymphoma, and less likely an infectious or reactive process. Given that she is a never-smoker of East Asian descent, HPV-associated oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma must be strongly considered, as this entity commonly presents with a level II nodal metastasis from a primary base of tongue or tonsillar lesion, sometimes preceding or accompanying subtle primary site symptoms.

The failure of symptoms to resolve with antibiotics, the character of the lymph node (firm, non-tender, non-fluctuant, approximately 2 cm), the referred otalgia, and the chronicity of unilateral pharyngeal symptoms are all features that warrant immediate further evaluation. Infectious lymphadenopathy is considered less likely given the non-tender, firm, non-fluctuant quality of the node and the lack of any acute infectious symptoms or fever.

- URGENT referral placed to otolaryngology (head and neck surgery) today. Patient advised this is an urgent referral and that she should be seen within the next 1-2 weeks. Given the clinical picture, I have marked this as urgent/priority in the referral system.
- CT scan of the neck with IV contrast ordered today to better characterize the extent of lymphadenopathy and evaluate for any identifiable primary site lesion in the pharynx and oropharynx. Radiology will contact patient to schedule as soon as possible.
- Patient counseled extensively at today's visit regarding the findings. I explained that while the diagnosis is not yet confirmed, the constellation of symptoms and exam findings is concerning enough to expedite evaluation. She was understandably anxious and tearful during this discussion. I emphasized that we do not yet have a diagnosis, that many causes are possible, and that early evaluation is key.
- Patient was instructed to go to the emergency department if she develops progressive dysphagia, respiratory difficulty, stridor, or rapid enlargement of the neck mass.
- Given symptom burden, patient may continue OTC analgesics (acetaminophen) as needed for throat and ear discomfort in the interim.

2. DISPOSITION:
- Follow-up: to be seen by otolaryngology as soon as possible. Patient will return to this office as needed or following specialist evaluation.
- Patient given written summary of today's visit and referral information.
- After-hours instructions provided.

Time spent in counseling and coordination of care: approximately 25 minutes of a 40-minute visit.

[Electronically signed by: Attending Physician, MD — Internal Medicine / Primary Care]

HEMATOLOGY/ONCOLOGY CONSULTATION NOTE
Head and Neck Multidisciplinary Oncology Program

---

REFERRING SERVICE: Otolaryngology / Head and Neck Surgery
REASON FOR CONSULTATION: New diagnosis of HPV-positive squamous cell carcinoma of the left base of tongue; tumor board review and treatment planning.

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Ms. Margaret Chen is a 52-year-old East Asian woman, never-smoker, presenting for medical oncology consultation following a new diagnosis of HPV-positive squamous cell carcinoma of the left base of tongue with ipsilateral level IIA nodal involvement. She was referred following multidisciplinary head and neck tumor board review for treatment planning discussion.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Chen initially presented to her primary care physician several months ago with approximately 3 months of persistent left-sided throat discomfort and a sensation of something stuck in the back of her throat. She had mild pain with swallowing but was able to eat solid foods without significant difficulty. There was no hoarseness and no ear pain at that time. She was treated empirically with amoxicillin-clavulanate for presumed pharyngitis; symptoms did not improve. She returned approximately 2 months later with continued throat discomfort, now accompanied by left-sided otalgia. On that visit, her PCP noted a firm, non-tender approximately 2 cm left neck mass and referred her urgently to Otolaryngology.

Subsequent workup included a CT neck with contrast demonstrating a 2.1 x 1.8 cm left level IIA cervical lymph node with internal heterogeneity and loss of fatty hilum, and asymmetric fullness of the left base of tongue. MRI neck confirmed a T2-hyperintense, gadolinium-enhancing left base of tongue mass measuring 2.2 x 1.9 x 1.7 cm, with the left level IIA node measuring 2.1 x 1.8 cm with central necrosis; no radiographic extranodal extension was identified. PET-CT (18F-FDG) demonstrated an FDG-avid left base of tongue primary (SUVmax 12.4) and a single hypermetabolic left level IIA lymph node (SUVmax 9.8); no contralateral nodal or distant metastatic disease was identified.

Direct laryngoscopy with biopsy confirmed squamous cell carcinoma, moderately differentiated, arising from the left base of tongue with diffuse strong p16 overexpression (>70% tumor nuclei and cytoplasm, block positive) and HPV type 16 confirmed on in situ hybridization. FNA of the left level IIA node showed metastatic squamous cell carcinoma, also p16 positive. Molecular profiling by 500-gene targeted NGS panel showed PIK3CA p.E545K activating mutation (VAF 28%), TRAF3 truncating mutation, KMT2D loss-of-function mutation, EP300 frameshift, FBXW7 p.R465C, FAT1 truncating mutation, and NOTCH1 truncating mutation; TP53 was wild-type, consistent with HPV-related disease. PD-L1 CPS was 18. TMB 7.2 mut/Mb. No EGFR, KRAS, or FGFR alterations. MSI-stable.

The case was presented at our head and neck multidisciplinary tumor board today. Surgical, radiation oncology, medical oncology, pathology, radiology, and speech-language pathology were all represented. After review of all imaging, pathology, and molecular data, the consensus of the tumor board was to recommend definitive concurrent chemoradiation with curative intent. Surgical resection was discussed but not recommended: for T2N1 HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC in a never-smoker, function-preserving concurrent chemoradiation achieves excellent disease control rates comparable to surgery, with lower rates of long-term dysphagia and without the morbidity of transoral robotic surgery or neck dissection in this clinical context.

Patient was seen today in consultation to discuss the treatment plan in full.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
1. No prior malignancies
2. No known autoimmune disease
3. No prior head and neck surgery or radiation
4. Hypothyroidism — not yet diagnosed (see below; at-risk given planned neck radiation)
5. Denies hypertension, diabetes, coronary artery disease, chronic kidney disease

PAST SURGICAL HISTORY:
- Direct laryngoscopy with biopsy (recent, per Otolaryngology)
- No other significant surgical history

SOCIAL HISTORY:
Ms. Chen works as a high school music teacher. She is married and lives at home with her spouse and two adult children who are both supportive. She is a lifelong never-smoker. Alcohol use is minimal (fewer than 1 drink per week). She denies illicit drug use. She is of East Asian descent. No occupational exposures of concern.

FAMILY HISTORY:
No family history of head and neck cancer. No known hereditary cancer syndromes. Father had hypertension; mother alive and healthy.

ALLERGIES:
No known drug allergies.

MEDICATIONS:
1. No regular prescription medications prior to this diagnosis
2. Occasional ibuprofen PRN for headache
3. Multivitamin daily

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
Positive for: persistent left-sided throat discomfort and odynophagia (mild, able to swallow soft foods and liquids), left-sided otalgia (referred), palpable left neck mass (noted by PCP and ENT). Mild fatigue.

ASSESSMENT

Ms. Chen is a 52-year-old never-smoker with newly diagnosed HPV-positive, p16-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma of the left base of tongue (T2N1M0, Stage III) presenting for pre-treatment swallowing evaluation. At baseline, she demonstrates only mild pharyngeal dysphagia, characterized by mildly reduced left-side tongue base retraction and mild vallecular pooling without aspiration, consistent with direct tumor involvement of the left base of tongue. Functional oral intake is normal with no diet restrictions required at this time. Nutritional status is adequate.

She is scheduled to begin definitive IMRT (70 Gy, 35 fractions, bilateral neck fields) with concurrent high-dose cisplatin. Based on her planned radiation fields — which include bilateral neck and oropharynx with full-dose coverage of the primary site and level II–IV cervical nodes — she is at high risk for treatment-related dysphagia. Expected acute toxicities include severe mucositis, odynophagia, and progressive impairment of tongue base retraction, pharyngeal constriction, and cricopharyngeal function secondary to radiation-induced edema and mucositis. Long-term risks include radiation fibrosis of pharyngeal constrictors, reduced epiglottic inversion, cricopharyngeal dysfunction, and xerostomia — all of which can contribute to chronic dysphagia and aspiration risk.

Given the degree of planned radiation to the pharyngeal musculature and bilateral neck, nutritional supplementation will almost certainly be required during treatment. The possibility of PEG tube placement was discussed in detail.

---

PLAN

1. Baseline Swallowing Function: Documented as described above — mild pharyngeal phase dysfunction attributable to primary tumor, no aspiration, FOIS 7. This will serve as the functional baseline for serial monitoring throughout treatment.

2. Pre-treatment Swallowing Exercises: Patient educated on and instructed to perform a prophylactic swallowing exercise program to be maintained throughout and after radiation, including:
   - Effortful swallow
   - Mendelsohn maneuver
   - Tongue base retraction exercises (tongue press against hard palate with posterior push)
   - Jaw-opening exercises for trismus prevention (Therabite device prescription placed; she was fitted and instructed today)
   - Shaker head-lift exercise (daily progressive protocol)
   The rationale for prophylactic exercises was explained: evidence suggests that maintenance of swallowing musculature activity during and after radiation may reduce the severity of long-term dysphagia and fibrosis.

3. Patient Education and Counseling: Ms. Chen was counseled extensively regarding:
   - The expected course of swallowing function during treatment: initial worsening typically begins in weeks 2-3 of CRT, with peak severity in weeks 4-6 and post-treatment period. She was reassured that most patients with HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC achieve meaningful functional recovery.
   - Mucositis management: sodium bicarbonate and saline rinses, avoidance of alcohol-based mouthwashes, magic mouthwash for comfort.
   - Importance of maintaining some oral intake (even small amounts) throughout treatment to preserve swallowing coordination — "use it or lose it" principle explained.
   - Dietary texture progression and modifications that may be helpful as mucositis develops (soft, moist foods; avoidance of acidic, spicy, or rough-textured items).
   - Signs of aspiration to report: coughing or choking with meals, recurrent fever, shortness of breath, wet or gurgly voice quality after eating.
   - Xerostomia: expected to be significant and prolonged; artificial saliva, xylitol gum, frequent hydration discussed.
   - Voice: she was counseled that temporary voice changes are expected and that her professional vocal demands as a music teacher will need to be considered in post-treatment rehabilitation planning.

4. PEG Tube Discussion: Prophylactic PEG tube placement was discussed in detail, including indications, procedure, and likely benefit during peak treatment toxicity. Ms. Chen was informed that, given bilateral neck irradiation, moderate-to-severe mucositis is anticipated and that nutritional supplementation is expected to be necessary during the treatment course. After thorough discussion of risks (tube dislodgement, infection, granuloma formation, potential for dependency) and benefits (maintained nutrition, hydration, medication delivery), the patient elected to defer prophylactic PEG tube placement at this time. She expressed understanding of the risks of significant weight loss and dehydration during treatment and agreed to a low threshold for revisiting PEG placement if oral intake becomes inadequate (defined as inability to maintain >75% estimated caloric needs orally, or weight loss >5% from baseline). She was given clear instructions on when to call for urgent re-evaluation and expedited PEG placement.

5. Nutritional Baseline Documentation: Pre-treatment weight 62 kg, BMI 22.1, albumin 4.1 g/dL. Dietary consultation co-management is recommended and referral placed; caloric goal estimated at 1800-2000 kcal/day during treatment.

6. Serial Monitoring During Treatment: SLP follow-up scheduled at approximately weeks 3, 5, and 7 of chemoradiation to assess functional status, oral intake adequacy, and need for intervention. Patient and family given direct contact information for urgent concerns.

7. Post-treatment Follow-up: Formal reassessment of swallowing function with repeat instrumental study (MBSS or FEES) planned at approximately 8-12 weeks post-completion of chemoradiation to characterize post-treatment functional status and guide rehabilitation.

Patient expressed understanding of all of the above and had her questions answered. She is appropriately counseled and motivated. She will bring her husband to her next SLP visit as requested.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Chen is a 53-year-old never-smoking woman of East Asian descent with a history of p16-positive, HPV-16-confirmed T2N1M0 (AJCC 8th edition, stage III, HPV-positive staging) squamous cell carcinoma of the left base of tongue, who presents today for her 18-month post-treatment surveillance visit.

To briefly summarize the pertinent history: the patient was diagnosed approximately 18 months ago with moderately differentiated squamous cell carcinoma of the left base of tongue with a single left level IIA metastatic lymph node (2.1 cm), confirmed on direct laryngoscopy biopsy and FNA cytology. Molecular profile was notable for HPV-16 positivity on ISH, p16 overexpression on IHC, PIK3CA p.E545K hotspot mutation (VAF 28%), TRAF3 truncating mutation, KMT2D loss-of-function mutation, and FBXW7 mutation; TP53 was wild-type consistent with the HPV-positive phenotype. TMB was intermediate at 7.2 mutations/Mb, PD-L1 CPS 18. Given these genomic findings and the T2N1 disease burden, enrollment on a de-escalation protocol was discussed at multidisciplinary tumor board but ultimately not pursued; standard curative-intent definitive IMRT (70 Gy in 35 fractions to the primary site and gross nodal disease, with elective nodal irradiation bilaterally) with concurrent cisplatin 100 mg/m2 every 3 weeks was recommended and administered.

The treatment course was complicated by significant toxicity: she developed grade 3 confluent oropharyngeal mucositis by approximately week 5 of chemoradiation, requiring PEG tube placement for nutritional support, as well as grade 2 nephrotoxicity (creatinine peak 1.32 mg/dL from baseline 0.78 mg/dL) that resulted in omission of the third cisplatin cycle. She received two of three planned cisplatin cycles (cumulative dose 340 mg/m2). Radiation was completed without interruption at full prescribed dose. She subsequently achieved a complete metabolic response on 12-week post-treatment PET-CT (SUVmax at primary site declined from 12.4 to 1.8; left level IIA node down to 0.9 cm with no pathologic FDG avidity). PEG tube was removed approximately 8 weeks after completing treatment. 6-month and 12-month surveillance imaging showed no recurrent disease; the 12-month PET-CT was entirely clear.

At today's 18-month surveillance visit, Ms. Chen reports she is feeling extremely well overall. She has returned to full-time work as a high school music teacher, which she finds gratifying and reports as a meaningful marker of her functional recovery. She denies any new throat discomfort, odynophagia, dysphagia, hoarseness, otalgia, or sensation of a lump in the throat. She is tolerating a completely normal diet without restriction. She reports persistent, mild xerostomia — her primary ongoing complaint — which she manages with frequent water sips throughout the day and occasional use of Biotene oral rinse. Her dose was uptitrated to 75 mcg daily at a subsequent visit, and she reports feeling well on this regimen — no fatigue, no cold intolerance, no constipation or other hypothyroid symptoms. She is compliant with her medication.

She denies any new neurological symptoms, weight loss, hemoptysis, dyspnea, or bone pain. No new skin lesions or palpable masses noted by the patient. ECOG performance status 0.

CLINICAL INDICATION: 52-year-old female with 3-month history of left throat discomfort and odynophagia, now with a firm palpable left level II cervical lymph node approximately 2 cm on physical examination. Rule out malignancy. Referral from ENT.

ASSESSMENT
Ms. Chen is a 52-year-old never-smoking East Asian woman presenting with a 4-month history of left throat discomfort, referred left otalgia, and a firm left level IIA cervical lymph node. Flexible nasopharyngoscopy today reveals an exophytic, friable, contact-bleeding lesion arising from the left base of tongue, estimated at approximately 2 cm, with an associated 2 cm firm left level IIA node confirmed on both imaging and bimanual palpation.

The clinical and radiologic picture is highly suspicious for oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma, primary base of tongue. Given the patient's demographics -- never-smoker, East Asian woman in her early 50s -- and the endoscopic appearance of this lesion, HPV-associated oropharyngeal SCC must be strongly considered as the leading diagnosis. The differential diagnosis at this point remains narrow; lymphoma is on the differential given the prominent nodal involvement, but the endoscopic appearance of the primary lesion is far more consistent with squamous cell carcinoma. Salivary gland malignancy (e.g., mucoepidermoid carcinoma or adenoid cystic carcinoma of the base of tongue) is possible but less likely given the gross morphology.

Histologic confirmation is required before any treatment planning proceeds.

---

PLAN

1. BIOPSY: The patient has been counseled regarding the suspicious nature of the left base of tongue lesion and the need for tissue diagnosis. She will be scheduled for direct laryngoscopy with biopsy under general anesthesia. At the time of surgery, systematic examination of the upper aerodigestive tract will be performed to assess extent of disease and rule out synchronous primaries. Fine needle aspiration (FNA) of the left level IIA cervical lymph node will be performed concurrently (or intraoperatively) for cytologic evaluation.

2. ADDITIONAL STAGING IMAGING: Cross-sectional imaging of the chest (CT chest with contrast, or PET-CT) will be obtained to evaluate for distant disease, pending and likely contingent on pathologic confirmation. We anticipate requesting PET-CT for comprehensive staging given the clinical scenario.

3. MULTIDISCIPLINARY CARE: If malignancy is confirmed on biopsy, the case will be presented at our multidisciplinary head and neck tumor board. Medical oncology, radiation oncology, speech-language pathology, and dietetics consultations will be arranged as indicated.

4. PATIENT COUNSELING: Ms. Chen was informed of today's examination findings, including the discovery of an abnormal lesion at the left base of tongue. The findings were explained in detail using plain language; she was accompanied by her husband. The possibility of malignancy was discussed honestly and with compassion. She was informed that tissue biopsy is the necessary next step before any definitive statements about diagnosis or treatment can be made. She was understandably distressed but composed, asking thoughtful questions about timeline, prognosis, and the implications for her voice and swallowing function. She was reassured that these concerns will be central to our treatment planning process. She verbalized understanding and agreement with the proposed plan.

5. RETURN: Patient to be scheduled for direct laryngoscopy and biopsy within 1 week. She will be contacted with a confirmed surgical date by the end of the business day.

GROSS DESCRIPTION

Specimen A (Left Base of Tongue Biopsy):
Received in formalin, labeled with the patient's name and "left base of tongue biopsy," are four fragments of tan-pink, firm, granular tissue ranging from 0.2 to 0.6 cm in greatest dimension. Aggregate tissue measures 1.2 x 0.8 x 0.3 cm. The specimen has a rough, irregular surface and lacks a discernible submucosal layer on gross inspection. Entirely submitted in one cassette (A1), with smaller fragments wrapped in tissue paper for additional sections.

Specimen B (Left Level IIA Lymph Node FNA):
Received as three air-dried and alcohol-fixed smears and one RPMI liquid-based sample, labeled "left level IIA FNA." Material is cellular, with grossly visible tissue fragments. Cell block prepared from residual RPMI material and submitted in one cassette (B1).

---

MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION

Specimen A — Left Base of Tongue Biopsy:
Multiple fragments of squamous mucosa are present, all showing architectural and cytologic features of malignancy. The surface epithelium is largely replaced by irregular nests, sheets, and cords of atypical squamous cells infiltrating a desmoplastic stroma. The tumor cells display moderate to abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm, enlarged vesicular nuclei with prominent nucleoli, and irregular nuclear membranes. Intercellular bridges (desmosomes) are focally identified, supporting squamous differentiation. Individual cell keratinization is present focally; however, well-formed keratin pearls are absent, consistent with moderate (non-keratinizing to partially keratinizing) differentiation. Mitotic figures are frequent, including atypical forms. There is a prominent tumor-infiltrating lymphocyte (TIL) response at the tumor-stromal interface, a feature characteristic of HPV-associated oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma. No perineural invasion or lymphovascular invasion is identified in the available biopsy material, though the specimen is fragmentary and assessment is limited.

Specimen B — Left Level IIA FNA:
The air-dried Diff-Quik and alcohol-fixed Papanicolaou-stained smears are highly cellular. There are cohesive and loosely dispersed clusters of malignant epithelial cells with features consistent with poorly to moderately differentiated squamous cell carcinoma. The cells exhibit high nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratios, irregular nuclear contours, and coarse chromatin. Background lymphocytes are abundant. The cell block (B1) demonstrates metastatic carcinoma with the same morphologic features. No necrotic debris or granulomas identified.

---

IMMUNOHISTOCHEMICAL AND ANCILLARY STUDIES

The following immunohistochemical (IHC) stains were performed on formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded sections from Specimen A (block A1) using automated platforms with validated controls:

p16 (CDKN2A surrogate marker for HPV infection):
Result: DIFFUSE STRONG BLOCK POSITIVE. Strong nuclear and cytoplasmic immunoreactivity in >70% of viable tumor cells, satisfying criteria for positivity per College of American Pathologists (CAP)/ASCO guideline. This pattern is consistent with transcriptionally active high-risk HPV infection.

CK5/6 (Cytokeratin 5/6):
Result: POSITIVE. Diffuse cytoplasmic positivity in tumor cells, confirming squamous lineage.

p40 (deltaNp63):
Result: POSITIVE. Strong nuclear staining in tumor cells, confirming squamous differentiation and supporting primary squamous cell carcinoma over metastatic adenocarcinoma or other carcinomas.

CK7:
Result: FOCAL WEAK POSITIVE. Seen in a minority of tumor cells; does not suggest adenocarcinoma in context.

CK20:
Result: NEGATIVE.

p53:
Result: WILD-TYPE PATTERN (scattered low-level positivity, non-overexpressed). Consistent with TP53 wild-type genotype; in keeping with HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC.

Ki-67 (MIB-1 proliferation index):
Result: Approximately 60% of tumor cell nuclei positive, reflecting a high proliferative rate consistent with HPV-associated SCC.

HPV In Situ Hybridization (ISH), High-Risk HPV Cocktail (Types 16/18/31/33/45/52/58):
Platform: Ventana INFORM HPV III Family 16 Probe (B) high-risk probe cocktail.
Result: POSITIVE. Punctate and diffuse nuclear hybridization signals are identified within tumor cell nuclei in >75% of tumor cells, confirming the presence of transcriptionally active high-risk HPV within the neoplastic squamous cell population.

HPV Genotyping by ISH (Type-Specific Probes):
HPV-16 specific probe: POSITIVE. HPV-18 specific probe: NEGATIVE.
Interpretation: High-risk HPV type 16 confirmed as the infecting viral genotype.

FNA Cell Block (Specimen B1) — p16 IHC:
Result: DIFFUSE STRONG POSITIVE. Identical pattern to primary tumor, confirming p16/HPV-positive metastatic squamous cell carcinoma.

---

DIAGNOSIS

A. LEFT BASE OF TONGUE, BIOPSY:
   — SQUAMOUS CELL CARCINOMA, MODERATELY DIFFERENTIATED (NON-KERATINIZING TYPE)
   — Immunohistochemistry: p16 DIFFUSE STRONG POSITIVE (block positive, >70% tumor cells); CK5/6 positive; p40 positive; consistent with HPV-related oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma.
   — HPV In Situ Hybridization: HIGH-RISK HPV POSITIVE (type 16 confirmed).
   — Ki-67 proliferation index: approximately 60%.
   — Note: Perineural and lymphovascular invasion status is indeterminate due to limited biopsy material.

B. LEFT LEVEL IIA CERVICAL LYMPH NODE, FINE NEEDLE ASPIRATION:
   — POSITIVE FOR MALIGNANT CELLS; CONSISTENT WITH METASTATIC SQUAMOUS CELL CARCINOMA, P16 POSITIVE.
   — Morphology and immunoprofile are consistent with spread from the known left base of tongue primary.

---

COMMENT

The histomorphologic features, in combination with the immunohistochemical profile (diffuse strong p16 block positivity, CK5/6 and p40 expression, TP53 wild-type pattern) and confirmatory high-risk HPV ISH (type 16), are diagnostic of HPV-associated oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma arising from the left base of tongue. This entity is recognized as a biologically and clinically distinct form of head and neck squamous cell carcinoma, predominantly affecting younger, non-smoking patients and showing a more favorable prognosis compared to HPV-negative disease. The finding of metastatic squamous cell carcinoma in the left level IIA lymph node establishes regional nodal involvement. Per AJCC 8th edition guidelines for HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma, p16 block positivity on IHC (with or without confirmatory HPV ISH) is required for classification under the favorable HPV-positive staging system; both criteria are met in this case.

Correlation with clinical and radiologic findings is recommended. Results have been communicated to the referring otolaryngology team.

MAGNETIC RESONANCE IMAGING — NECK WITH AND WITHOUT CONTRAST

EXAMINATION: MRI neck with and without gadolinium contrast
INDICATION: 52-year-old woman with newly diagnosed HPV-positive squamous cell carcinoma of the left base of tongue (T2N1M0, Stage III). Pre-treatment planning evaluation. Assessment of primary tumor extent, deep tissue involvement, and regional nodal disease.
TECHNIQUE: Multiplanar, multisequence MRI of the neck performed on a 3.0 Tesla magnet. Sequences include sagittal T1, axial T1, axial T2 with fat saturation, axial diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) with apparent diffusion coefficient (ADC) mapping, axial and coronal post-contrast T1 with fat saturation, and coronal STIR. Gadolinium-based contrast (gadobutrol 7.5 mL) administered intravenously without adverse reaction. Images reviewed in three planes.

COMPARISON: CT neck with contrast (obtained approximately 3 days prior).

FINDINGS:

PRIMARY TUMOR — OROPHARYNX / BASE OF TONGUE:
There is a well-defined mass arising from the left lateral aspect of the base of tongue. On T2-weighted sequences, the lesion demonstrates heterogeneous but predominantly T2-hyperintense signal relative to the adjacent tongue musculature. On DWI, there is conspicuous restricted diffusion (low ADC values estimated at approximately 0.78 x 10⁻³ mm²/s), consistent with densely cellular tumor. Following gadolinium administration, the mass shows brisk, avid, and heterogeneous enhancement. The lesion measures approximately 2.2 x 1.9 cm in the axial plane and 1.7 cm in the craniocaudal dimension.

The inferior margin of the lesion is at the level of the vallecula but does not extend into the vallecula or the pre-epiglottic fat space, which appears preserved. The lesion does not cross the midline; the right base of tongue, right lingual tonsil, and right lateral pharyngeal wall are all grossly normal in signal and enhancement pattern. Posteriorly, no clear invasion of the posterior pharyngeal wall musculature is identified. The superior margin of the lesion abuts but does not convincingly infiltrate the left tonsillar fossa; there is no discrete mass in the left tonsillar pillar or soft palate. The deep margin of the lesion shows irregular T2-hyperintense infiltration of the superficial fibers of the left hyoglossus muscle, but the genioglossus and intrinsic tongue musculature of the midline appear uninvolved. No involvement of the floor of mouth is identified. The mucosa overlying the lesion is thickened and irregular. There is no macroscopic erosion of the mandible or hyoid bone. The sublingual space and submandibular space are clear bilaterally.

REGIONAL LYMPH NODES:
Left neck: A single pathologically enlarged lymph node is present at the left level IIA station, measuring 2.1 x 1.8 cm in axial dimensions. The node demonstrates loss of the normal fatty hilum, irregular capsular margins, and internal signal heterogeneity on T2-weighted imaging with a focal T2-hyperintense, T1-hypointense center consistent with central necrosis. Post-contrast imaging confirms peripheral rim enhancement surrounding a non-enhancing necrotic core. The external capsule of the node appears intact on all sequences; the fat planes between the node and the adjacent sternocleidomastoid muscle are preserved. No discrete radiographic evidence of extranodal extension is identified, though the capsular margins are slightly irregular and warrants careful attention at surgical or radiation planning review.

No additional enlarged or morphologically abnormal lymph nodes are identified in the left level IB, III, IV, or V stations. No left retropharyngeal lymphadenopathy is identified.

Right neck: No pathologically enlarged or morphologically abnormal lymph nodes in any right-sided level. A few sub-centimeter nodes are noted in the right level II station, all with preserved fatty hila and no restricted diffusion; these appear reactive.

Retropharyngeal space: No enlarged retropharyngeal lymph nodes bilaterally.

SKULL BASE AND PARAPHARYNGEAL SPACE:
The skull base marrow signal is normal with no focal T1 hypointensity or T2 hyperintensity to suggest infiltration. Foramina of the skull base appear intact. The bilateral parapharyngeal fat spaces are preserved. The bilateral carotid arteries and internal jugular veins are patent, without encasement or displacement by tumor or adenopathy. The bilateral masticator spaces, parotid spaces, and pterygomandibular raphe are normal.

LARYNX AND HYPOPHARYNX:
The supraglottis, glottis, and subglottis are unremarkable. The epiglottis, aryepiglottic folds, and arytenoids are symmetric and normal in signal. No laryngeal or hypopharyngeal mass. The pyriform sinuses are symmetric and clear. The pre-epiglottic space contains normal fat signal.

SALIVARY GLANDS:
The bilateral parotid and submandibular glands are symmetric in size and signal intensity. No focal nodule or ductal obstruction.

VASCULAR STRUCTURES:
The bilateral common carotid arteries, internal and external carotid arteries, and internal jugular veins are patent and demonstrate normal flow voids. No vascular encasement.

VERTEBRAL COLUMN AND PREVERTEBRAL SPACE:
The cervical vertebral bodies demonstrate normal marrow signal without evidence of osseous involvement. Disc spaces are maintained. No prevertebral soft tissue thickening or signal abnormality.

THYROID GLAND:
The thyroid gland is normal in size and signal. No discrete nodule above the threshold of detection.

IMPRESSION:
1. Left base of tongue mass, measuring approximately 2.2 x 1.9 x 1.7 cm, with restricted diffusion and avid gadolinium enhancement, consistent with the known primary squamous cell carcinoma. The lesion is confined to the left lateral base of tongue with superficial involvement of the left hyoglossus fibers. No extension across the midline, no vallecula or pre-epiglottic space involvement, and no floor-of-mouth or mandibular involvement identified.
2. Single left level IIA cervical lymph node with central necrosis and peripheral rim enhancement, measuring 2.1 x 1.8 cm, consistent with metastatic nodal disease. The external capsule appears grossly intact without definitive extranodal extension on this examination, though irregular capsular contour is noted.
3. No contralateral cervical lymphadenopathy.
4. No involvement of the retropharyngeal nodes, skull base, carotid sheaths, or prevertebral space.
5. Overall findings are concordant with the staging CT and recent PET-CT; primary tumor remains confined to the left base of tongue with a single ipsilateral nodal metastasis.

METHODOLOGY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Platform: Targeted next-generation sequencing, 500-gene panel (Illumina NovaSeq)
Sequencing Coverage: Mean on-target depth: 742x (tumor); variant calling threshold: minimum 5% VAF at ≥100x coverage
Analysis: Detection of somatic single-nucleotide variants (SNVs), insertions/deletions (indels), copy number alterations (CNAs), and gene fusions/rearrangements. Microsatellite instability (MSI) assessed using 114 microsatellite loci. Tumor mutational burden (TMB) calculated from somatic non-synonymous coding mutations per megabase (Mb) across the coding regions of the panel. PD-L1 combined positive score (CPS) reported from concurrent IHC performed on the same specimen block (22C3 antibody, Dako).
Limitations: This assay detects somatic alterations in the targeted gene regions only. Deep intronic variants, low-level mosaicism below detection threshold, and balanced translocations not in the target panel may not be detected. Results should be interpreted in the context of clinical and pathologic findings.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
INTERPRETATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
The somatic mutational profile of this HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma is consistent with published genomic characterizations of this disease subtype (TCGA HNSCC cohort; Gillison et al.). Key findings and their significance are as follows:

1. PIK3CA p.E545K (Exon 9 Hotspot Mutation) + PIK3CA Low-Level Copy Gain:
   This is a well-characterized oncogenic hotspot mutation in the helical domain of PIK3CA, encoding the p110-alpha catalytic subunit of PI3-kinase. The p.E545K variant constitutively activates downstream AKT/mTOR signaling. PIK3CA mutations occur in approximately 20-35% of HPV-positive OPSCCs and are among the most common oncogenic driver events in this setting. Concurrent low-level copy gain at 3q26 may further enhance PI3K pathway output. This finding is of potential clinical relevance as PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway inhibitors (e.g., alpelisib, copanlisib) are under investigation in head and neck cancers harboring PIK3CA alterations.

2. TRAF3 p.R242* (Truncating Mutation, VAF 41%):
   TRAF3 encodes a negative regulator of the NF-kB and type I interferon pathways. Truncating mutations in TRAF3 are enriched in HPV-positive HNSCC (occurring in ~10-17% of cases) and are thought to facilitate viral immune evasion and NF-kB pathway activation. The high VAF (41%) suggests this may be a clonal, early event. TRAF3 alterations are not directly actionable with approved therapies but provide context for immune microenvironment biology.

3. KMT2D p.Q1555* (Truncating Mutation, VAF 35%):
   KMT2D (also known as MLL4) is a histone H3K4 methyltransferase with tumor suppressor function. Loss-of-function mutations are among the most frequent epigenetic alterations across many cancers, including HNSCC. KMT2D loss can impair chromatin remodeling and transcriptional regulation. No approved targeted therapies directly target KMT2D loss, though preclinical data suggest sensitivity to certain HDAC inhibitors.

4. EP300 p.S1415fs*14 (Frameshift Deletion, VAF 19%):
   EP300 encodes the transcriptional coactivator p300, a histone acetyltransferase. Frameshift mutations result in loss of function. EP300 mutations co-occur with KMT2D alterations in HNSCC and together reflect a pattern of epigenetic dysregulation. The relatively lower VAF (19%) may indicate a subclonal event.

5. FBXW7 p.R465C (VAF 22%):
   FBXW7 is an E3 ubiquitin ligase substrate receptor that targets oncoproteins including Cyclin E, c-Myc, Notch, and c-Jun for proteasomal degradation. The p.R465C variant occurs at one of the three canonical hotspot arginine residues (R465, R479, R505) in the WD40 substrate-binding domain, impairing ubiquitin-mediated degradation of oncogenic substrates. Not directly actionable with approved agents.

6. FAT1 p.E2386* (Truncating Mutation, VAF 31%):
   FAT1 is a protocadherin with tumor suppressor function that modulates Wnt/beta-catenin and Hippo/YAP signaling. FAT1 inactivating mutations are found in approximately 15-25% of HNSCC. Loss of FAT1 may promote YAP nuclear translocation and oncogenic transcriptional programs. Preclinical data suggest potential sensitivity to YAP/TEAD inhibitors, none of which are currently approved.

7. NOTCH1 p.R2382* (Truncating Mutation, VAF 18%):
   NOTCH1 truncating mutations in HNSCC are thought to represent loss of tumor suppressor function (distinct from activating NOTCH1 mutations seen in T-ALL). Loss of NOTCH1-mediated differentiation signaling may contribute to squamous cell carcinoma progression. No approved targeted therapies.

8. TP53 Wild-Type:
   The absence of TP53 mutation is consistent with HPV-positive OPSCC, in which HPV E6 oncoprotein mediates p53 degradation via the ubiquitin-proteasome pathway. TP53 mutations are hallmarks of HPV-negative, tobacco-associated HNSCC but are uncommon in HPV-positive disease.

9. KRAS, EGFR, FGFR, CCND1 — Wild-Type:
   No alterations in these commonly targeted genes were detected. Absence of EGFR amplification is expected in the HPV-positive subtype. KRAS mutation would be highly atypical in this histologic and etiologic context.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
ACTIONABILITY TIERS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Tier I — Alterations with established clinical significance / FDA-approved therapies or companion diagnostic implications:

  PIK3CA p.E545K
    Alteration: Activating hotspot mutation, helical domain
    Potential significance: PI3K pathway activation; investigational PI3K/AKT/mTOR inhibitors (e.g., alpelisib, copanlisib, gedatolisib) are being evaluated in PIK3CA-altered HNSCC in clinical trials. No FDA-approved PI3K inhibitor indication exists specifically for HNSCC at this time; however, this finding may be relevant for enrollment in basket trials or PIK3CA-selected studies. Additionally, may influence response to de-escalation strategies (some data suggest PIK3CA mutation is associated with slightly higher recurrence risk in HPV-positive OPSCC).
    Standard-of-care implication: Identification of PIK3CA mutation noted as potentially relevant to risk stratification in HPV-positive OPSCC de-escalation trial eligibility.

  PD-L1 CPS 18 (Positive, <20)
    Potential significance: In the recurrent/metastatic HNSCC setting, Keytruda (Keytruda) monotherapy is FDA-approved for PD-L1 CPS ≥1 tumors, and pembro + platinum/5-FU is approved for CPS ≥1. CPS 18 confirms PD-L1 positivity. This is relevant if disease were to recur in a metastatic setting. In the current curative-intent setting, PD-L1 status does not change standard management.

  MSI/TMB:
    MSI-Stable; TMB intermediate (7.2 mut/Mb). TMB-high (≥10 mut/Mb) threshold required for tumor-agnostic Keytruda approval (KEYNOTE-158) is not met. MSS status also does not support checkpoint inhibitor monotherapy on a biomarker-only basis.

Tier II — Alterations with potential clinical significance; evidence from clinical trials, case series, or strong preclinical data:

  TRAF3 p.R242* — Loss of negative NF-kB regulation; no approved agent; research context only
  KMT2D p.Q1555* — Epigenetic loss of function; potential preclinical rationale for HDAC inhibitor sensitivity
  EP300 p.S1415fs*14 — Histone acetyltransferase loss; epigenetic dysregulation
  FBXW7 p.R465C — Impaired oncoprotein degradation; loss of tumor suppressor function
  NOTCH1 p.R2382* — Loss of differentiation signaling; tumor suppressor context

Tier III — Alterations of uncertain or limited clinical significance:

  FAT1 p.E2386* — Tumor suppressor loss, Hippo/Wnt pathway; not yet actionable with approved agents

Tier IV — Benign / likely benign variants:
  None reported in this summary.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
CLINICAL GENOMICS SUMMARY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
This HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma demonstrates a mutational landscape consistent with the HPV-positive HNSCC molecular subtype: TP53 wild-type, PIK3CA helical domain hotspot mutation, and co-occurring truncating mutations in TRAF3, KMT2D, EP300, FBXW7, FAT1, and NOTCH1. The most immediately clinically relevant finding is the PIK3CA p.E545K activating mutation, which activates the PI3K/AKT/mTOR axis and may have implications for trial eligibility or risk stratification in the HPV-positive OPSCC setting. PD-L1 CPS of 18 confirms immunologic expression relevant to checkpoint inhibitor use in the recurrent/metastatic setting should that circumstance arise. TMB is intermediate and MSI is stable.

Results should be correlated with clinical, radiologic, and pathologic findings. This report does not constitute a treatment recommendation. Clinical interpretation and management decisions remain the responsibility of the treating physician.
